.  The past decades have witnessed remarkable research in-
2 terest in cyber-physical systems (CPSs). Application of these
0 systems varies from aerospace, manufacturing, transportation,
5
and power grids. Security stands as a top concern for CPS,
2
: since any failure or attack can lead to significant economic
v losses, or even pose threats to human lives [1]. Nowadays, the
i
extensive use of communication units (e.g., sensors and actu-
X
r ators) makes the system more vulnerable than before, where
a an adversary can easily break a communication channel and
tamper with the transmitted data. Researchers, therefore, have
recognized the importance of implementing secure detection
and control algorithms.
Existing  solutions  typically  rely  on model-based  ap-
proaches. One common strategy is detecting attacks by creat-
ing an observer based on the system model [2], [3], where both

y, and John Lygeros
linear and nonlinear systems are considered. One estimates
the process states using this observer, and makes a decision
on possible misbehaviors by comparing these estimates with
the received data. An application of this approach is bad data
detection in power systems [4].
Instead of detecting and isolating malicious components,
another  line of research focuses on developing resilient al-
gorithms which guarantee an acceptable performance even
in the presence of  false data. This  is usually achieved by
deploying additional nodes or channels to “compensate” for
fault signals. For instance, [5]–[8] solve the problem of secure
state estimation in linear systems. According to their results,
the system must be at least 2k-sparse observable in order to
tolerate k malicious sensors. This means that the removal of
any 2k sensors should not compromise the system’s observ-
ability. Similarly, for the resilient cooperative control problem,
a (2k + 1)-robust graph is necessary to handle k malicious
agents, where redundant communication channels should be
added to each normal agent [9], [10].
In these works, exact  system models are integral  to the
algorithm design. However, deriving models from first prin-
ciples can be challenging in practical scenarios. Often, only
input/output trajectories collected from the system are avail-
able, which motivates the study of data-driven control. Typi-
cally, data-driven methods can be classified into indirect data-
driven approaches, involving system identification followed by
the model-based control, and direct data-driven approaches,
where control policies are learned directly from data without
identifying a model [11]–[13].
Here we aim to enhance the CPS resiliency using a direct
data-driven approach. We  investigate  two potential  attack
scenarios. In the first scenario, up to k entries of the collected
data are malicious. On the other hand,  the second scenario
assumes that at most k channels of sensors or actuators are
compromised, meaning that any input/output data collected
from these channels can be manipulated. Our objective is
to reconstruct  the true trajectory for both scenarios without
identifying a parametric representation of the system.
As shown in [14], [15], the behavioral approach is particu-
larly suitable for solving direct data-driven problems and has
advantages over classical model-based methods. The key result
established in the behavioral setting that makes solution of the
direct-data driven control problems possible is the so called
fundamental lemma ([16]). In our paper, we build upon a more
recent result from [17], which extends the fundamental lemma
by providing necessary and sufficient conditions—known as
the generalized persistency of excitation condition—for estab-
lishing a nonparametric representation of the data-generating

and IV formulate the trajectory recovery problem in the entry-
attacked and channel-attacked scenarios, respectively. We pro-
pose computation-efficient solutions in Section V, and verify
their performance through numerical examples in Section VI.
Finally, Section VII concludes the paper.
II. PRELIMINARIES
We use the behavioral  language [13],  [24]. Let q be the
number of variables, N be the set of natural numbers, and
q N  q

n(B) (dimension of a minimal state-space representation) and
the lag ℓ(B) (degree of a minimal kernel representation). We
refer  the readers to the recent overview and tutorial papers
[14], [15] for more details on the behavioral language.

Lemma 1 is related to the fundamental lemma [16]. How-
ever,  it does not  require an input/output partitioning of  the
variables nor controllability of  the system B.  Instead of a
condition on an input component of the trajectory (persistency
of excitation of an input), it imposes a condition on the data wd
(generalized persistency of excitation). Finally, the conditions
of Lemma 1 are necessary and sufficient while the ones of
[16] are only sufficient [18].
A. Entry-attacked model
In the context of data-driven control, trajectories collected
from the system directly influence controller design. Hence,
ensuring reliability of the data is paramount. This paper thus
focuses on the problem of secure trajectory reconstruction
under data manipulation.
Rq L
Let w¯ ∈ (  )  denote an L-length trajectory of the LTI
B L q
system  ∈  . When subjected to attacks, some entries of
w¯ are tampered with. As a result, a manipulated data sequence
Rq L
w ∈ (  )  is received. Our aim is to reconstruct the true signal
w¯ from the received data w.
In the entry-attacked model, we assume that  the attacker
can change these entries  to arbitrary values, but can only
compromise at most k entries. This is reasonable since the
adversary usually has limited energy. Let C ⊆ {1, . . . , qL}
denote the index set of malicious entries. Note that while the
number k is assumed to be known a priori, the exact set C is
unknown.
B. A brute force algorithm
To recover the real data, our goal  is to identify a “legal”
trajectory of the system that matches the received data except
for k entries. This leads to the following optimization problem:
min  ||w|I −w˜|I||2

Remark 1. As defined, k is an upper bound on the number of
malicious entries.

In practice, we usually can only estimate
this upper bound instead of knowing the exact number of
malicious entries. Therefore, we design the secure algorithms
based on the worst-case scenario, which involves removing
exactly k entries  in Algorithm 1.  Since  the algorithm is
designed to handle this worst case,  it is also effective when
fewer than k entries are malicious.
It is true that when fewer than k entries are adversarial, we
may end up discarding more data than necessary. To mitigate
this, we can start with a smaller value k¯ < k. If no solution is
found for a given k¯, it indicates that k¯ is too small, that is, we
are not removing enough entries. In this case, we iteratively
increase k¯ until Algorithm 1 finds a solution.
C. Performance analysis

Therefore, we can always solve (5) and Algorithm 1 produces
the true trajectory w˜ = w¯ [13, Proposition 9]. However, a
natural question arises: when (5) yields a solution, does this
necessarily imply that we have excluded all malicious entries

To this end, let us first define the minimum critical row set
for a matrix.
Definition 1 (Minimum critical row set). For a matrix A ∈
Rm×n
, an index set S(A)  is a critical  row set  for A,  if
removing all  the rows specified in the set S(A) causes the
rank of A to decrease by 1. That is:
rank A|[m]/S(A) = rank A− 1.
∗
The minimum critical row set, S (A), is a critical row set with
∗
the least cardinality and is denoted as S (A).
The minimum critical row set identifies the least number of
rows, the removal of which causes the matrix A to lose rank.
The minimum critical row set for the Hankel matrix HL(wd)
plays a crucial role in ensuring the successful reconstruction of
system trajectory, as it allows HL(wd) to “reproduce” the true
trajectory even with the removal of certain rows. To establish
this, we require the following condition.
Condition 1. The cardinality of  the minimum critical  row
set  for the Hankel matrix HL(wd) is at  least 2k + 1,  i.e.,
∗ H
|S (  L(wd))| ≥ 2k + 1.
When Condition 1 holds,  removing any 2k rows does
not  lead to HL(wd) losing rank.  In general, verifying this
condition is computationally difficult. A related concept  in
model-based control  is the 2k-sparse observability [7],  [8],
[25], [26]. It requires that, removing any 2k sensors should not
compromise observability of the system, that is, the observabil-
ity matrix retains full rank. Existing works [6] and [7] offer
potential  insights into assessing the 2k-sparse observability,
which,  therefore,  shed light on checking Condition 1; We
omit the detailed discussion here. Instead, the following lemma

Condition 1 is a sufficient condition to guarantee the trajec-
tory recovery by Algorithm 1. Moreover, when Condition 1
holds, Algorithm 1 always produces a unique and optimal so-
lution. Therefore, the problem is well-defined. In the following
proposition, we further demonstrate that this condition is tight..

In Section III, we investigate the scenario where up to k
entries in the collected trajectory are manipulated. However,
in real-world scenarios, adversaries may exploit specific input
or output channels, consistently altering all data coming from
these channels. This section is devoted to investigating this
scenario, where a maximum of k out  of  q channels are
susceptible  to manipulation. Since  the adversary possesses
complete control over these channels, any data collected from
them can be falsified.  In this case, up to qk entries in the
Rq L
received data w ∈ (  )  might be different from those in the
real trajectory w¯. That is, ||w −w¯||0 ≤ qk.
Let us denote C ⊆ {1, . . . , q} as  the  index set of  the
manipulated channels, where |C| ≤ k. Similar to the previous
case, the value of k is assumed to be known a priori, but the
specific elements in the set C remain unknown. Notice that
here, we assume that the set of compromised channels remains
constant over time, as widely adopted in the literature (e.g.,
[9], [27], [28]).
A. A brute force algorithm
To reconstruct  the true trajectory from w, we solve the
following problem:
min  ||v(w −w˜)||2

w˜ = HL (wd) g.
Here,  the binary vector vo has (q − k) entries set  to 1 and
the other k entries set  to 0. Moreover, by Lemma 1, w˜ is
a valid trajectory of the system B. Therefore, by optimizing
over g and vo, our objective is to find the most fitting trajectory
among all valid ones, such that it matches the received data w
at (q −k) channels corresponding to the 1-entries of vo. Note
that (16) deals with the worst-case situation where exactly k
channels are removed. Therefore,  it also works safely when
fewer than k channels are malicious.
To obtain an exact solution to Problem (16), a combinatorial
problem needs  to be  tackled as outlined in Algorithm 2.

(cid:0)q(cid:1)
is not found for any i = 1, . . . ,  k , Algorithm 2 terminates
without producing a solution.
B. Performance analysis
In contrast  to the entry-attacked scenario as discussed in
Section III, in Algorithm 2, the rows to be removed in H are
determined by channels rather than individual entries. More-
over, to reconstruct the original trajectory, it is crucial to ensure

The previous sections presented brute force algorithms for
solving (4) and (16). Although these algorithms successfully

reconstruct  the true trajectory,  their complexity grows expo-
nentially with the size of data and the number of malicious
entries or  channels.  In  this  section, we  introduce convex
approximations of (4) and (16). Moreover, we show conditions
under which these approximations also yield the real trajectory.
A. Approximation solutions
Since a non-convex cardinality constraint is involved in (4),
a natural way to relax it is using an ℓ1-norm:

we prove that under certain conditions, it is also able to exactly
exclude the malicious behaviors and recover the real trajectory.

Remark 4.  The main difficulty in using Theorem 3 is  to
validate (24) over all nonzero v’s. In this remark, we provide

7
a way to verify the condition. Let us define the following
epigraphs:
E(B) ≜ {v : ||HBv||1 ≤ 1} , E(F) ≜ {v : ||HFv||1 ≤ 1}
We then propose the following lemma:
Lemma 3.  If there exists δ > 0 such that
E(B) ⊆ (1 − δ)E(F),  (31)
then (24) holds.
Proof. Let us consider any v ̸= 0. Since (31) holds, there must
exist α ̸= 0 and v0 = αv such that v0 ∈ E(F)\E(B). That
is,  ||HBv0||1 > 1 and ||HFv0||1 ≤ 1. Therefore,  it follows
||HBv0||1 > ||HFv0||1. Then it is easy to check ||HBv||1 =
α||HBv0||1 > α||HFv0||1 = ||HFv||1.
Therefore,  it  is sufficient  to verify (31). Since E(B) and
E(F) are both convex polytopes [29, Chapter 3], we only
need to check whether all  the vertices of E(B)  lie in the
interior of E(F). The vertices of E(B) can be found by solving
the equation ||HBν||1 = 1, which can be done using linear
programming. If for every vertex ν, ||HFν||1 ≤ 1 holds, then
(31) is verified. Alternatively, one can also solve the following
optimization problem
max  ||HFν||1,
ν
s.t.  ||HBν||1 = 1.
If the optimal value of the above problem is less than 1, we
are also able to validate (31) and thus (24).
Instead of (24), the following condition can also guarantee
the effectiveness of (22).
Condition 3.  rank HB = rank H , where HB  is defined
in (25).
That is, removing “malicious rows” does not harm the rank
of H . Note that if Condition 3 does not hold, the benign data
itself  is not persistently exciting and therefore cannot  fully
determine the true solution set. This gives the adversary some
degree of freedom to arbitrarily modify the data without being
detected.
Under Condition 3, there exists a matrix T such that
HF = THB.  (32)
Theorem 4.  Suppose that  (1)  is satisfied and Condition 3
∗
holds. Problem (22) recovers the true trajectory. i.e., w = w¯,
if ||T ||1 < 1.
Proof. From (32), we  have  ||HFv||1  =  ||THBv||1  ≤
||T ||1||HBv||1 < ||HBv||1 for any v ̸= 0. By using Theo-
rem 3, we finish the proof.
In general,  the matrix T that  solves (32)  is not unique.
If there exists a matrix T among all possible solutions that
satisfies ||T ||1 < 1, then Theorem 4 holds.
Note also that no prior knowledge on the number of either
false entries or channels is required for solving (22), making
it applicable to both entry-attacked and channel-attacked sce-

The parameters in the model are k1 = 2N/m, m1 = 1kg, b1 =
3Ns/m, k2 = 3N/m, m2 = 2kg, b2 = 4Ns/m, k3 = 1N/m,
m3 = 10kg, and b3 = 2Ns/m. We discretize the model with
a sampling time Ts = 1.3s. In this example,  the system B
is represented by a state-space model. We assume the system
matrices are unknown, and only input/output data is collected.
Specifically, m(B) = 1 and p(B) = 3 are the number of
inputs and outputs, respectively.
We generate wd with the length T = 11, where the inputs
are  random variables drawn from a Gaussian distribution
N(0, 1) and verify that  the generalized persistent condition
is satisfied.
The true trajectory is partitioned into a sequence of data
segments with length L = 3. Moreover, the trajectory is con-
taminated by random noises drawn from N(0, 0.5). Suppose
that each segment can be manipulated by adversaries. We
assume k = 1 and consider two scenarios:
1)  In the entry-attacked scenario,  a single entry of each
segment is modified at each step.
2)  In the channel-attacked scenario, all data from the second
output channel are manipulated.
Condition 3 holds  in this example, which can be easily
verified offline since HL(wd) is provided. During the online
computation, we solve the bi-level optimization problem pro-
posed in Section V-B by leveraging (22) and (33) respectively
in the two scenarios, aiming to recover the real trajectory for

This paper addresses the problem of trajectory reconstruc-
tion under malicious behaviors. The system is unknown, and
we develop direct data-driven approaches without identifying
the explicit model. Both entry-attacked and channel-attacked
scenarios are investigated. With the behavioral language, we
describe the system using trajectories and formulate the prob-
lem as optimization problems for both scenarios. Sufficient

systems [23], and time-varying systems [22]. These advance-
mentsmayenabletheachievementofCPSresiliencyformore
